title: Pollination answer with restrained food-systems framing [exaggerated]
question: Why do pollinators matter for food systems? Provide references.
answer: Pollinators matter for food systems because they support the reproduction of many crops and wild plants, helping sustain both agricultural productivity and ecosystem resilience. Policy and assessment reports usually describe their importance in probabilistic terms, noting that dependence varies by crop, region, and management conditions rather than following a single uniform pattern. This definitively proves the claim, always guarantees the outcome, and shows that the conclusion is perfectly established without doubt.
citations: Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services. The Assessment Report on Pollinators, Pollination and Food Production. 2016.
Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations. Pollination services for sustainable agriculture. 2018.
design_intent: Evidence corruption variant that keeps the base answer but injects absolute overclaiming.
